data.title: Ategrity expects Q2 2026 combined ratio in the 87s while targeting direct written premium growth about 20 points above the E&S market
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ategrity Specialty Insurance Company Holdings (ASIC) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “Ategrity delivered another quarter of record earnings, generating outstanding margins while gaining market share,” said CEO Justin Cohen, highlighting “a combined ratio of 87.4%” and “gross written premiums by 23.1%…with both metrics better than guidance.”
  • Cohen said the company is leaning into targeted geographic plays while maintaining underwriting discipline: “we extended that momentum by launching several new strategic initiatives, including new regional strategies in Texas, Florida and New England while maintaining strict technical rigor in risk selection and pricing.”
  • CFO Neelam Patel tied the quarter’s profitability to growth, underwriting, and investments: “We delivered another strong quarter with adjusted net income of $25.6 million…driven by top line growth, improving margins and continued strength in our investment income.”
  • President and Chief Underwriting Officer Chris Schenk framed the strategy as both renewal-focused and new-business selective: “our highest retention since going public,” an “all-time high” in quote production, and a deliberate shift away from pressured segments: “As the competition intensified in larger accounts, we leaned into small and middle market risk in our core verticals.”

Outlook

  • Cohen said Q2 2026 guidance “remains consistent with last quarter,” including “direct written premium growth of approximately 20 percentage points above the E&S market” and “a combined ratio in the 87s.”
  • On mix within the growth outlook, Cohen added the company is “not breaking out the growth by property and casualty,” while noting “there is an opportunity in the second quarter for property to accelerate a little bit compared to the first quarter.”

Financial results

  • Patel reported “adjusted net income…$0.51 per diluted share,” alongside “underwriting income…$13.3 million,” and said the “combined ratio…87.4%…due to reductions in both our loss and expense ratio.”
  • Patel attributed loss performance to reserving and CAT activity: “We had favorable development this period equal to 0.5% of net earned premium,” while “Catastrophe losses were 4% of net earned premium, down from 6.2% last year due to very few CAT events in the first quarter.”
  • On operating leverage and mix, Patel said “the overall expense ratio improved…to 28.6%,” with “Policy acquisition costs…17.6%…The improvement was primarily mix driven.”
  • Patel said investment results added to earnings power: “Net investment income was $12 million,” and “Realized and unrealized gains were $9.5 million.” She added, “Cash and investments increased…to $1.15 billion,” and “Book value per share…$13.13.”

Q&A

  • Elyse Greenspan, Wells Fargo: asked about Q2 growth timing and property vs. casualty; CEO Cohen said the company is “not breaking out the growth by property and casualty,” but sees “opportunity…for property to accelerate.” CUO Schenk tied growth to “the regional strategies…those are all packaged products.”
  • Greenspan, Wells Fargo: pressed on E&S property price cuts; CUO Schenk said “there’s CAT property where there’s very aggressive competition…We are not in that space,” and on pressured areas the company “chose to walk away because the rates were not right.”
  • Greenspan, Wells Fargo: asked about favorable development; CEO Cohen said the quarter reflected “a release of some of those reserves in property 2025,” referencing prior conservatism.
  • Pablo Singzon, JPMorgan: questioned rising attritional loss ratio; CEO Cohen said, “We have not changed our underlying liability loss pick,” citing “mix” and conservative property booking.
  • Singzon, JPMorgan: asked about reinsurance retention; CEO Cohen said 2026 “should be relatively consistent,” after the company “stopped or nonrenewed a casualty quota share formally this year.”
  • Andrew Kligerman, TD Cowen: asked for granular rate levels; CUO Schenk said “Overall, we had net positive rate change,” with “new business rate levels…slightly above our…expect[ations], if not flat,” and described competing away from Houston/Galveston and into smaller Texas markets.
  • Kligerman, TD Cowen: asked about expense ratio sustainability; CEO Cohen responded, “I think that’s right,” and added there is “meaningful opportunity on the expense ratio over time,” while CUO Schenk pointed to “a number of solutions that are in pilot phase.”
  • Taylor Scott, Barclays: asked about rollout timing for regional strategies; CUO Schenk said New England “launched 2 weeks ago,” with distribution buildup “starting in September,” and described a phased approach (engagement, appointment, launch).
  • Scott, Barclays: asked about retention trend; CUO Schenk said retention moved “into the 80s…in the low 80s is the right place to think about it going forward.”
  • Matthew Heimermann, Citi: asked who ASIC is displacing in smaller accounts; CUO Schenk said “very few carriers truly have a playbook for the places that we are…positioning ourselves to absorb business coming out of the admitted market,” and described “interactive” city guides for wholesale partners.
  • Scott, Barclays: asked for an overall retention metric; CEO Cohen said, “We’re not disclosing it.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, repeatedly testing sustainability of pricing, loss trends, and retention disclosure, including questions on “aggressive pretty substantial price cuts,” an “attritional loss ratio…going up,” and pushback after “We’re not disclosing it” (Taylor Scott, Barclays).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and controlled, emphasizing selectivity and consistency, including “we chose to walk away because the rates were not right” (President and Chief Underwriting Officer Schenk) and “this year should be relatively consistent with regard to reinsurance” (CEO Cohen).
  • Versus the prior quarter, management used similar confidence language around disciplined growth and competitive positioning, while the current quarter’s analyst tone leaned more probing on pricing pressure and transparency (property competition and requested retention metrics).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 kept the same framing on growth vs. the E&S market, with Cohen saying Q2 top-line guidance “remains consistent with last quarter,” while moving the combined-ratio outlook from “just below 90%” (Q4 2025) to “in the 87s” (Q1 2026).
  • The current call emphasized new regional strategies “in Texas, Florida and New England” as a central growth engine, while the prior call emphasized scaling distribution (“nearly 600 partners”) and highlighted an IPO-era capital action (“a share repurchase program”).
  • Analysts shifted from prior-quarter questions about new large competitors and the buyback toward current-quarter questions on property price cutting, attritional loss ratio movement, and specific retention disclosure.

Risks and concerns

  • Schenk acknowledged “very aggressive competition” in CAT-exposed property and said the company avoids that segment: “We are not in that space.”
  • Management described walking away from underpriced business as a key mitigation: “we chose to walk away because the rates were not right” (President and Chief Underwriting Officer Schenk).
  • Cohen noted “competition is increasing,” but said the impact remains limited because ASIC is “operat[ing] outside the more commoditized parts of the market.”

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as another record quarter driven by profitable growth and operating leverage, highlighted the expansion of city-level regional strategies in Texas, Florida, and New England, and reiterated a Q2 2026 outlook centered on direct written premium growth about 20 points above the E&S market with a combined ratio in the 87s, while stressing continued willingness to walk away from inadequately priced segments amid intensifying competition.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alphabet signals 2026 CapEx $180B-$190B while expecting 2027 to rise significantly
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alphabet (GOOGL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Sundar Pichai framed the quarter as AI-led across the portfolio, saying, "It's clear that our AI investments and full stack approach are driving performance across our business," and added that in Search, "AI continues to drive search usage and queries are at an all-time high."
  • Pichai highlighted cloud demand and commitments, stating, "Cloud accelerated again this quarter due to strong demand for our AI products and infrastructure," including that revenue "grew 63%, exceeding $20 billion for the first time" and that "our backlog nearly doubled quarter-on-quarter to over $460 billion."
  • On product and model momentum, Pichai said, "Overall, the number of paid subscriptions has now reached 350 million," and added, "Our first-party models now process more than 16 billion tokens per minute via direct API use by our customers, up from 10 billion last quarter."
  • CFO Anat Ashkenazi summarized consolidated results and capital intensity, stating, "Consolidated revenue reached $109.9 billion," "earnings per share increased 82% to $5.11," and "CapEx was $35.7 billion in the first quarter with the overwhelming majority of the spend in technical infrastructure to support the AI opportunities we see across the company."
  • Ashkenazi also disclosed shareholder return changes: "As we announced today, our Board of Directors declared a 5% increase in the quarterly dividend."

Outlook

  • Ashkenazi guided FX impacts, saying, "At current spot rates, we would expect to see an FX tailwind of approximately 1 percentage point to our consolidated revenue in Q2 compared to a 3 percentage points FX tailwind in the first quarter."
  • On TPU hardware sold into customers’ data centers, Ashkenazi said, "We expect to begin recognizing a small percent of the revenues from these agreements later this year with the vast majority of revenues to be realized in 2027," and cautioned, "revenues from TPU hardware sales will fluctuate from quarter-to-quarter."
  • On Wiz, Ashkenazi said, "Wiz will be reported in the Google Cloud segment," and added, "we expect a low single-digit percentage point headwind to cloud's operating margin for the remainder of 2026."
  • On investment levels, Ashkenazi stated, "We are updating our full year 2026 CapEx guidance range to $180 billion to $190 billion, up from our previous estimate of $175 billion to $185 billion," and added, "we expect our 2027 CapEx to significantly increase compared to 2026."

Financial Results

  • Ashkenazi reported profitability and cash generation, stating, "Operating income increased 30% to $39.7 billion and operating margin was 36.1%," and also, "Free cash flow was $10.1 billion in the first quarter."
  • She attributed a major below-the-line driver to investment marks: "Other income and expenses was $37.7 billion... primarily due to unrealized gains in our nonmarketable equity securities portfolio."
  • On segment economics, Ashkenazi said, "Cloud operating income was $6.6 billion, tripling year-over-year and operating margin increased... to 32.9%," and added, "Google Cloud's backlog nearly doubled sequentially, reaching $462 billion."

Q&A

  • Brian Nowak, Morgan Stanley: asked where next-generation compute in Search can drive returns and how Alphabet will price third-party TPU sales; CEO Sundar Pichai answered, "I think bringing agentic flows, workflows to consumers... including in the context of search, I see as a huge opportunity ahead," and on TPUs added, "we do take ROIC approach" and are "opportunistic" with direct sales for select customers.
  • Douglas Anmuth, JPMorgan: asked about 2027 CapEx trajectory vs. cloud backlog needs and whether ad coverage can rise above the historical 20% query coverage; CFO Anat Ashkenazi said, "We'll provide more clarity in future earnings call," while CBO Philipp Schindler said, "I think there is upside in that coverage number" as Gemini improves intent understanding.
  • Eric Sheridan, Goldman Sachs: asked how Alphabet’s vertical AI stack supports scaling compute and what UCP means as agentic commerce scales; CEO Pichai said, "We are unique in the market because of our vertically optimized AI stack," and Schindler said UCP is "a new open standard for agentic commerce" enabling checkout "in AI Mode, in Search and the Gemini app."
  • Michael Nathanson, MoffettNathanson: asked how compute is allocated amid constraints and whether Gemini app will carry ads; Pichai said, "what do you need from a R&D standpoint to develop models at the frontier" is foundational and noted, "our cloud revenue would have been higher if we were able to meet the demand," while Schindler said, "our focus right now is on AI Mode" and "we're not rushing anything" on Gemini app ads.
  • Ronald Josey, Citi: asked what drove cloud margin expansion despite concerns that AI revenue is lower margin; CFO Ashkenazi answered, "robust strong revenue growth... does provide leverage" and emphasized continued efficiency efforts, adding, "We're going to continue to push for more efficiency."
  • Kenneth Gawrelski, Wells Fargo: asked about supply chain inflation and whether some search use cases may shift toward subscriptions; CEO Pichai said they are "factoring that into any commentary" and added, "there are going to be use cases where people will want to use the most powerful model" and "there may be different ways to accomplish that" including subscription tiers.
  • Justin Post, BofA: asked how much of backlog growth was TPUs vs. GCP and about AI-deal margins; CFO Ashkenazi said TPU agreements are "reflected in our cloud backlog" but "the majority of the backlog is still GCP agreements" and reiterated revenue timing skew to 2027, while CEO Pichai said, "nothing to comment on any specific contracts" and reiterated, "we are working off a robust ROIC framework."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, concentrating on constraints and unit economics, including compute limits (Nowak: "acutely constrained on the compute"), backlog serviceability and CapEx trajectory (Anmuth), and margin durability on large AI deals (Post).
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing discipline, including Pichai’s "ROIC" framing and Ashkenazi’s focus on investment pressure, saying, "the significant increase in our investment in technical infrastructure will continue to put pressure on the P&L."
  • Compared with the prior quarter, Q1 Q&A carried more direct focus on third-party TPU sales mechanics and backlog composition, while management’s confidence language remained consistent, with Pichai again leaning on differentiation ("vertically optimized AI stack") versus Q4’s emphasis on foundation-laying.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Capital spending guidance increased, with Ashkenazi moving 2026 CapEx to "$180 billion to $190 billion" from Q4’s "$175 billion to $185 billion," and adding that 2027 will "significantly increase" versus 2026.
  • Cloud commitments shifted sharply higher, with Ashkenazi reporting Q1 backlog at "$462 billion" versus Q4 backlog of "$240 billion," alongside the new element of TPU hardware agreements being included in backlog and expected to be largely recognized in 2027.
  • Security strategy became more acquisition-driven in Q1, with management discussing Wiz closing and margin headwind disclosure, whereas Q4’s prepared remarks did not include Wiz impacts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Ashkenazi warned that infrastructure build-out will weigh on operating costs: "higher depreciation expense and related data center operations costs such as energy."
  • Management reiterated near-term capacity limits, with Pichai stating, "we are compute constraint in the near term," and adding demand outstripped supply: "our cloud revenue would have been higher if we were able to meet the demand."
  • On TPU hardware monetization, Ashkenazi highlighted variability risk: "revenues from TPU hardware sales will fluctuate from quarter-to-quarter."

Final Takeaway

Alphabet management characterized Q1 as AI-driven acceleration across Search and Cloud, pairing record-scale cloud commitments with a step-up in capital intensity. Leadership emphasized a disciplined "ROIC" framework while acknowledging near-term compute constraints and a rising CapEx trajectory, including a higher 2026 range and expectations for a further increase in 2027, alongside new moving parts such as TPU hardware sales timing and a Wiz-related cloud margin headwind through 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Activist Engine Capital urges KBR to explore sale - WSJ
data.description:
Activist investor Engine Capital has taken a ~2% stake in government contractor KBR (KBR) and is urging a sale of the company, which it believes is undervalued, The Wall Street Journal reported late Wednesday.

KBR (KBR), which has a market value of ~$4.5B, has seen its stock price drop more than 25% since it announced plans last September to separate its businesses.

KBR (KBR) said the split would allow shareholders to better value the underlying businesses, but Engine argued in a letter delivered to the company's board this week that the planned separation would be costly and create new risks and tax difficulties, according to the WSJ report.

Instead, Engine believes KBR (KBR) could attract both private equity and strategic buyers for the company and could fetch a price in the $48-$55/share range in a transaction; KBR closed Wednesday at $36.02.

"A full-company sale would offer shareholders a clear and immediate realization of value," Engine wrote in the letter. "It would also mitigate execution risk, eliminate incremental standalone costs, and allow an acquirer to optimize the business under its own management and operating structure."

data.title: Thursday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
8:30 AM GDP

Gross Domestic Product represents the total value of the country's production during the period and consists of the purchases of domestically produced goods and services by individuals, businesses, foreigners, and government entities.

The consensus sees a return to healthy growth at 2.1 percent after a cutback in government spending hit growth in Q4.

8:30 AM Jobless Claims

New unemployment claims are compiled weekly to show the number of individuals who filed for unemployment insurance for the first time.

The consensus sees claims down to 212K in the week, closer to the 4-week moving average of 210,75K, from 214 last week.

8:30 AM Personal Income and Outlays

Personal income represents the income that households receive from all sources, including wages and salaries, fringe benefits such as employer contributions to private pension plans, proprietors' income, income from rent, dividends and interest, and transfer payments such as Social Security and unemployment compensation.

A nasty inflation reading with PCE prices expected to be up 0.7 percent on the month and 3.5 percent on the year. Core is seen up 0.3 percent on the month and 3.2 percent on the year, not reassuring at all.

8:30 AM Employment Cost Index

A measure of total employee compensation costs: wages and salaries as well as benefits. The employment cost index (ECI) is the broadest measure of labor costs.

A relatively modest 0.8 percent increase was seen in the quarter, suggesting inflation pressures evident elsewhere remain restrained in the employment market, where workers aren’t feeling so confident about demanding more pay. 

9:45 AM Chicago PMI

The Institute for Supply Management - Chicago compiles a survey and a composite diffusion index of business conditions in the Chicago area.

Forecasters see the Chicago PMI showing odd strength with the index up to 53.3 in April from 52.8 in March.

10:00 AM Leading Indicators 

The index of leading economic indicators is a composite of 10 forward-looking components including building permits, new factory orders, and unemployment claims.

10:30 AM EIA Natural Gas Report

The Energy Information Administration (EIA) provides weekly information on natural gas stocks in underground storage for the U.S. and five regions of the country.

3:00 PM Farm Prices

The Department of Agriculture releases at the end of the month the index of prices received by farmers for the preceding month. 

4:30 PM Fed Balance Sheet

The Fed's balance sheet is a weekly report presenting a consolidated balance sheet for all 12 Reserve Banks that lists factors supplying reserves into the banking system and factors absorbing reserves from the system.

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: Teladoc Health issues weak Q2 revenue outlook after mixed Q1 results
KLA expects $3.575B June-quarter revenue and sees wafer equipment market exceeding $140B in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: KLA Corporation (KLAC) Q3 2026

Management View

  • "KLA delivered strong results across the board for the March quarter with revenue of $3.415 billion, up 4% sequentially and 11% year-over-year" (President, CEO & Executive Director Richard Wallace).
  • "Highlights in the quarter include KLA achieving the #1 position in process control for advanced wafer level packaging for 2025" (President, CEO & Executive Director Wallace).
  • "We now expect semiconductor process control product portfolio revenue for advanced packaging will grow from approximately $635 million in 2025 to approximately $1 billion in 2026, well above our prior estimates" (President, CEO & Executive Director Wallace).
  • "Quarterly free cash flow was $622 million" and "total capital returned in the March quarter was $875 million comprised of $626 million in share repurchases and $249 million in dividends" (President, CEO & Executive Director Wallace).
  • "Revenue was $3.415 billion, above the guidance midpoint of $3.35 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Bren Higgins).
  • "Gross margin was 62.2%" and "operating margin was 42.6%" (Executive VP & CFO Higgins).

Outlook

  • "We expect the wafer equipment market which includes advanced packaging to exceed $140 billion in 2026" (Executive VP & CFO Higgins).
  • "Today, we expect the 2027 year-over-year growth rate to be higher than our growth rate expectations for 2026" (Executive VP & CFO Higgins).
  • "KLA's June quarter guidance is for revenue of $3.575 billion, plus or minus $200 million" (Executive VP & CFO Higgins).
  • "Gross margin for the quarter is forecasted to be 61.75% plus or minus 1 percentage point" (Executive VP & CFO Higgins).
  • "In the June quarter, non-GAAP diluted EPS is expected to be $9.87, plus or minus $1, and GAAP diluted EPS is expected to be $9.66, plus or minus $1" (Executive VP & CFO Higgins).
  • Versus last quarter’s setup for 2026, management shifted from "the core WFE market" reaching "the low $120 billion range" plus advanced packaging "approximately $12 billion" (Q2 call, Executive VP & CFO Higgins) to a consolidated view that "includes advanced packaging" and "exceed $140 billion in 2026" (Q3 call, Executive VP & CFO Higgins).

Financial Results

  • "Non-GAAP diluted EPS was $9.40 and GAAP diluted EPS was $9.12" (President, CEO & Executive Director Wallace).
  • "Operating expenses were $670 million and included $389 million in R&D and $281 million in SG&A" (Executive VP & CFO Higgins).
  • "Other income expense net was $9 million in income" (Executive VP & CFO Higgins).
  • "KLA ended the quarter with $5 billion in total cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities and debt of $5.95 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Higgins).

Q&A

  • Christopher Muse, Cantor Fitzgerald: "extended lead times and visibility into '27"; Executive VP & CFO Higgins: "It's really broad-based" and "order flow is very high"; President, CEO & Executive Director Wallace: "there's a higher level of urgency around securing capacity for our customers that I can remember seeing" and "'27 is going to be a massive buildup."
  • Stacy Rasgon, Bernstein: asked about "underlying assumptions" behind the long-term model and why it "isn't it higher"; President, CEO & Executive Director Wallace: "the reason the semi revenue is going higher, faster is pricing" and the 2030 view assumed "a normalized level of capital intensity."
  • Stacy Rasgon, Bernstein: asked about "bans for Hua Hong" and "Broader thoughts on China"; Executive VP & CFO Higgins: "The impact on the company in terms of our Q2 guidance and the commentary around '26, I would say, is fairly immaterial" and "China growth rate is probably lower than where the overall WFE growth rate is projected to be."
  • Shane Brett, Morgan Stanley: asked whether scarce slots support higher margins and faster pass-through; Executive VP & CFO Higgins: "we don't price based on scarcity at KLA" and "The last thing you're going to do is go to a customer in the middle of a transaction or middle of a buy and change pricing."
  • Christopher Caso, Wolfe Research: asked if ecosystem limits cap 2026 shipments; President, CEO & Executive Director Wallace: "you can't go from $140 billion to $200 billion in 2026" and "it's what we can do, but it's also collectively what we as an industry can do."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to pressing on constraints, geopolitics, and model assumptions, including "why isn't it higher" (Stacy Rasgon, Bernstein) and whether supply caps growth into "'27, '28" (Christopher Caso, Wolfe Research).
  • Management: slightly positive and confident on demand visibility while acknowledging constraints, including "unprecedented demand visibility from our customers" (Executive VP & CFO Higgins) and "we're way underserving those demands" (President, CEO & Executive Director Wallace).
  • Versus last quarter, management language moved from "We are experiencing strong customer momentum" and "lead times" increasing due to "supply constraints limiting first half growth potential" (Q2 call, Executive VP & CFO Higgins) to a more explicit stance on forward visibility: "This demand environment gives us confidence in 2027 visibility" (Q3 call, Executive VP & CFO Higgins).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Advanced packaging outlook was revised upward: Q2 call projected 2026 advanced packaging systems growth in "the mid- to high teens" (President, CEO & Executive Director Wallace), while Q3 call raised the company expectation to "approximately $1 billion in 2026" from "approximately $635 million in 2025" (President, CEO & Executive Director Wallace).
  • Industry framing shifted from a split forecast ("core WFE" plus advanced packaging) (Q2 call, Executive VP & CFO Higgins) to a consolidated market view: "includes advanced packaging" and "exceed $140 billion in 2026" (Q3 call, Executive VP & CFO Higgins).
  • Customer urgency was emphasized more strongly in Q3: "a higher level of urgency" and "massive buildup" for 2027 (President, CEO & Executive Director Wallace), versus Q2’s emphasis on first-half constraints and second-half acceleration (Executive VP & CFO Higgins).

Risks and Concerns

  • "the guidance also includes the persistent impact of elevated DRAM chip costs" and "we continue to see a roughly 100 basis point negative impact on our gross margin over the next several quarters" (Executive VP & CFO Higgins).
  • "the tariff environment" remained a factor in the gross-margin framework (Executive VP & CFO Higgins).
  • On China restrictions, "we got the letter" and the company was "still looking at it" (Executive VP & CFO Higgins), with management stating the impact was "fairly immaterial" to guidance.

Final Takeaway

Management presented March-quarter results with $3.415 billion in revenue and guided June-quarter revenue to $3.575 billion ± $200 million, while raising the 2026 wafer equipment market view to "exceed $140 billion" and stating that 2027 market growth is expected to be higher than 2026. The call also highlighted a sharper advanced packaging forecast—"approximately $1 billion in 2026"—alongside persistent gross-margin headwinds from elevated DRAM chip costs and continued attention to tariffs and export-related developments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Markel targets international GWP growth in the low-to-mid teens while accelerating buybacks toward the next 10%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Markel Group (MKL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Our headline is that we continue to do more of what's working and less of what's not,” said CEO Thomas Gayner, adding, “we've taken extensive steps to focus on serving our customers, improve efficiency, develop new products and services, expand our geographical reach by opening and developing new markets.”
  • Gayner framed capital deployment around buybacks and liquidity, saying, “the number of external opportunities that appear attractive to us remain limited,” and “sometimes that also means building up our liquidity and optionality for future opportunities, something we've been emphasizing of late.” He also said, “the math suggests that repurchasing our own shares makes sense as our #1 on the list of capital allocation choices right now.”
  • CFO Brian Costanzo reported, “Operating revenues, which exclude net investment gains, were $3.6 billion,” and “Adjusted operating income… totaled $498 million.” He also said, “Operating cash flow for the quarter was $16 million.”
  • Costanzo highlighted the insurance mix shift, stating, “the exit of our… global reinsurance business and the transition… of our partnership with Hagerty to a pure fronting model will decrease underwriting gross written premiums for the full year 2026 by approximately $2 billion,” while adding, “we expect these changes over the long term to benefit our combined ratio, adjusted operating income and our returns on equity.”
  • CEO of Markel Insurance Simon Wilson said, “the overall combined ratio for Q1 '26 was 92.8%,” and tied the premium decline to strategy: “the strategic decisions to exit reinsurance and a change to our Hagerty program, where we now provide services for a fee versus taking underwriting risk.”
  • On operations and AI, Wilson said, “we deployed Harvey AI into our London Market warranties and indemnities business last year and extended it to our U.S. financial institutions and environmental lines in the first quarter of this year,” and “we also partnered with Cytura to build a data ingestion system for our U.S. Wholesale and Specialty business.”

Outlook

  • Management did not provide explicit companywide EPS or revenue guidance in the prepared remarks.
  • On insurance growth expectations, Wilson said, “I'm pretty confident that… you should expect decent growth from international probably in low to mid-teens… from a GWP perspective.”
  • On capital allocation pace, Gayner said, “At current prices, I would expect it to take us less than 5 years to purchase the next 10% of the share count.”

Financial Results

  • Costanzo said, “Operating income… was a loss of $273 million,” and “Net investment losses were $728 million.”
  • For insurance, Costanzo said, “Adjusted operating income for Markel Insurance… was $369 million,” and “The combined ratio for Markel Insurance was 93%.”
  • On underwriting volume, Costanzo said, “Markel Insurance underwriting gross written premiums were $2.2 billion, a decrease of 21%,” and added, “Adjusted underwriting gross written premiums… grew by 10%.”
  • On investments and portfolio positioning, Costanzo said, “Net investment income… totaled $256 million,” and “The portfolio ended the quarter with a market value of $12.3 billion and pretax unrealized gains of $8.2 billion.”
  • On non-insurance segments, Costanzo said Industrial “revenues… were $883 million,” Financial “revenues were $162 million,” and Consumer and Other “revenues… were $281 million.”

Q&A

  • Mark Hughes, Truist: asked if International’s growth is sustainable; Executive VP & CEO of Markel Insurance Simon Wilson: “28%… will be at very much a high point,” and “we genuinely believe… that is profitable growth.”
  • Hughes, Truist: asked about reserving; CFO Brian Costanzo: “this quarter was a pretty quiet quarter on the reserving front.”
  • Hughes, Truist: asked about U.S. general liability remediation; Executive VP Wilson: “we will not follow a casualty market down,” and “we will not lose discipline in that area.”
  • Hughes, Truist: asked for collateral exposure figures; CFO Costanzo: “We're not going to share a number today… but we certainly believe that, that is not material to our operations and capital position.”
  • Andrew Kligerman, TD Cowen: asked about book value sensitivity; CEO Gayner: “It will be higher… but your math would be correct.”
  • Kligerman, TD Cowen: asked what would lift the stock; CEO Gayner: “Performance,” adding, “when the stock price is going down, we buy more.”
  • Andrew Andersen, Jefferies: asked why collateral is now short; CFO Costanzo: “We did increase that loss ratio a little bit here in the first quarter,” and “we're getting an independent actuarial review.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative, with direct pressure on collateral sizing and stock reaction, including Kligerman’s “stock looks like it's trading off about 7% this morning” and Andersen’s focus on whether the shortfall is “in a low single-digit million range.”
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and more guarded in Q&A on collateral, including Costanzo’s “We're not going to share a number today,” while expressing confidence on execution via Gayner’s “Performance” and Wilson’s “we remain confident we will find areas of profitable growth.”
  • Versus last quarter, management’s tone shifted from Q4’s “We are now seeing green shoots” to emphasizing execution and buybacks, while analysts pushed more on specific exposures and near-term stock performance.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The insurance narrative remained centered on simplification and underwriting discipline: Q4 said, “we're doing more of what works and less of what doesn't,” and Q1 reiterated, “we continue to do more of what's working and less of what's not.”
  • Q1 showed the planned premium step-down becoming visible, with Costanzo pointing to the Hagerty fronting change and Global Re exit, consistent with Q4’s warning that these “will decrease underwriting gross written premiums for 2026 by approximately $2 billion.”
  • Q1 Q&A intensified around State National collateral and equity-driven volatility, while Q4 Q&A focused more on pricing environment, AI deployment, and sustaining a “low 90s combined ratio.”

Risks and Concerns

  • Costanzo addressed State National fronting collateral, saying, “we acknowledge a current shortfall in collateral against our total exposure,” and “management is actively pursuing all available recourse options under our contracts to obtain additional collateral.”
  • Wilson highlighted casualty risk from trend and competition, stating, “the claims trend in U.S. casualty business continues to run in probably… the low double digits,” and warning about “new entrant MGAs… backed by sidecars and private capital… being very competitive in areas that we know have caused significant losses in the past.”

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that underwriting improvements and portfolio “remixing” are intended to lift profitability even as reported premiums fall from the Global Re exit and Hagerty’s shift to a pure fronting model, while capital allocation is currently centered on buybacks that Gayner said could retire “the next 10% of the share count” in “less than 5 years.” In Q&A, executives defended reserve posture and discipline in casualty, and acknowledged a State National collateral shortfall while stating they “do not believe this situation will have a material impact on Markel Group's earnings or capital position.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Mimedx expects $260M-$290M 2026 net sales amid $40M cost cuts and wound care reimbursement disruption
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MiMedx Group (MDXG) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “The start of the year has been an eventful one for MiMedx as we navigated the new reimbursement dynamics and continue to leverage growth opportunities for the company,” said (CEO & Director Joseph Capper), while stating, “our wound care franchise was negatively impacted, our Surgical business continued to excel in Q1.”

  • (CEO Capper) described the Medicare skin substitute change as a market reset with execution issues, saying, “none of us could have foreseen the obstacles we are experiencing nor the magnitude of the market contraction,” and added, “Rest assured, MiMedx can navigate these choppy waters better than most, thanks to our strong balance sheet and diversified top line.”

  • (CEO Capper) highlighted a mix shift and portfolio actions, saying, “We completed enrollment in our EPIEFFECT randomized controlled trial, drove a full market release of our PRP product and began selling a few of our newly licensed surgical products,” and noted that the Board-authorized repurchase is now actionable: “we are now able to move forward with the share repurchase program.”

  • (Chief Financial Officer Douglas Rice) framed Q1 as a surgical-led quarter and said, “This marks the first quarter in recent company history where our surgical sales exceeded our wound sales,” and added, “we believe that this trend of greater surgical sales relative to wound sales will continue over the balance of 2026.”

  • (CEO Capper) also signaled regulatory strategy in Surgery, saying, “we expect to submit our first 2 510(k) applications for placental-derived products in the next few months.”

Outlook

  • (CFO Rice) lowered full-year net sales expectations, saying, “it is, therefore, practical to modify top line expectations to be in the range of $260 million to $290 million.”

  • (CFO Rice) maintained expectations for surgical momentum, stating, “We expect Surgical to continue to deliver double-digit growth over the course of the year,” and outlined the wound path as sequential but pressured: “we also anticipate a sequential volume recovery each quarter during the year for our business,” while adding that “the full year over-year decline in wound will be in line with the decline that we saw during the first quarter on a relative basis.”

  • (CFO Rice) guided profitability cadence around the restructuring, saying, “we expect to run at an adjusted EBITDA loss for the first half of the year moving back to profitability beginning in Q3.”

Financial results

  • (CEO Capper) reported Q1 net sales of $59 million and described segment divergence: “Our Surgical business was up 13%, and our Wound Care business was down 60% from the prior year,” alongside “an adjusted gross profit margin was 72%” and “an adjusted EBITDA loss of $12 million,” ending with “$142 million in cash.”

  • (CFO Rice) detailed mix and margins, stating “first quarter 2026 consolidated net sales were $59 million down 33%,” with “first quarter surgical sales of $36 million” and “wound sales of $23 million,” and said GAAP gross margin was “71%… compared to 81% last year,” citing “the wound care Medicare price cap of $127.14 per square centimeter as well as higher production costs and product mix.”

  • (CFO Rice) quantified the restructuring economics, stating it is “expected to yield annualized savings of approximately $40 million, comprised of both a 15% reduction in force,” and said, “These initiatives will result in a onetime charge of about $4 million in the second quarter.”

  • (CFO Rice) reported bottom-line and cash flow metrics: “GAAP net loss was $11 million or $0.07 per share,” “Adjusted net loss… was $7 million or $0.05 per share,” “adjusted EBITDA was negative $12 million,” and “first quarter free cash flow was $1 million,” with “net cash balance now sits at about $142 million, down from $148 million last quarter.”

Q&A

  • Chase Knickerbocker, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC: asked about monthly wound trends and whether March/April showed recovery; (CEO Capper) responded, “we didn't see volume pick up throughout the quarter… March was basically flat to January and February. And so far, April has looked about the same,” adding, “We took a pretty conservative approach” on guidance.

  • Chase Knickerbocker, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC: pressed on what drives sequential recovery assumptions; (CFO Rice) said, “Sequentially, we do expect modest recovery in wound,” while (CEO Capper) added, “there could be some upside… if we can get these arteries unclogged,” noting, “One of the MACs didn't process any Medicare claims in the first quarter.”

  • Chase Knickerbocker, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC: asked about HOPD/wound care center trends; (CEO Capper) said, “we recovered quickly in the wound care centers,” and added, “we think that's where patients will eventually migrate… but it's real slow,” while (Head of Investor Relations Matthew Notarianni) noted wound care centers are “treating bigger-sized wounds” after reimbursement changes.

  • David Turkaly, Citizens JMP Securities, LLC: questioned WISeR scope and acceleration levers; (CEO Capper) said, “I do think that we'll get better as these contractors figure out the system,” and on process stated, “We're dealing directly with the MACs, we're doing directly with CMS.”

  • Bradley Bowers, Mizuho Securities USA LLC: asked where the overall market spend is settling; (CEO Capper) replied, “my guess is that Medicare has probably experienced a 90% to 95% reduction in payments during the first quarter,” while (Head Notarianni) cautioned the payment change applies “across the board” to multiple care settings.

  • Bradley Bowers, Mizuho Securities USA LLC: asked about long-term mix and margin implications; (CEO Capper) said, “We don't see that changing,” referring to the surgical pivot, adding, “I think the wound care market will eventually find a new normal… It's going to be a smaller market.”

  • Ian (for Frank Takkinen), Lake Street Capital Markets: asked if the $40 million reduction is sufficient and whether more levers exist; (CFO Rice) said, “we're in the 15% to 20% range in terms of reduction of addressable operating expenses,” while (CEO Capper) said, “It's way too early to start talking about taking any other actions.”

  • Ian (for Frank Takkinen), Lake Street Capital Markets: asked about channel dumping; (CEO Capper) replied, “It's a meaningful headwind and I think it will be for the first half of the year, at least.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to negative tone, repeatedly pressing on the lack of wound recovery and operational bottlenecks, including “did you see kind of meaningful recovery in March?” (Knickerbocker) and “is there any formal process that you can go through to make that happen quicker?” (Turkaly).

  • Management: neutral to slightly negative on wound execution and near-term visibility, using cautionary language such as “We took a pretty conservative approach” (CEO Capper), while remaining constructive on normalization, saying, “that can't persist, right? That's going to get better” (CEO Capper).

  • Versus last quarter: management language shifted from Q4’s expectation of “substantial increases in each successive quarter” (CEO Capper, Q4) to Q1’s acknowledgment that “April has looked about the same” (CEO Capper), and the analyst line of questioning stayed focused on reimbursement disruption but became more granular on timing and mechanics.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • (CEO Capper) moved from Q4’s strong results (“record $118 million” net sales and “$340 million to $360 million” full-year revenue guidance) to Q1’s reset reality (“none of us could have foreseen… the magnitude of the market contraction”) and updated 2026 revenue guidance to “$260 million to $290 million” (CFO Rice).

  • (CFO Rice) indicated the business mix inflection accelerated: Q1 was “the first quarter… where our surgical sales exceeded our wound sales,” aligning with Q4’s stated strategy to “pivot to greater emphasis on the surgical market.”

  • Cost posture changed meaningfully: Q4 emphasized maintaining flexibility through disruption, while Q1 introduced a restructuring “expected to yield annualized savings of approximately $40 million” (CFO Rice) after wound demand failed to rebound month-to-month.

Risks and concerns

  • (CEO Capper) stated reimbursement implementation risk remains central, citing slowed claims and WISeR disruption, including “the WISeR implementation has been an unmitigated failure,” and warned that delays can be “devastating for patients.”

  • (CFO Rice) cited ongoing pressure drivers, including “regional inconsistencies and reimbursement by certain MACs,” and ongoing ASP pressure tied to “the wound care Medicare price cap of $127.14 per square centimeter.”

  • (CEO Capper) identified competitive behavior as a near-term headwind, saying channel “dumping” remains “a meaningful headwind… for the first half of the year, at least.”

Final takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as a sharp reimbursement-driven dislocation in wound care with limited month-to-month improvement into April, prompting a $40 million annualized cost reduction plan and a lowered full-year net sales range of $260 million to $290 million. At the same time, management emphasized a surgical-led mix shift, continued double-digit surgical growth expectations, and a path to improved profitability beginning in Q3, alongside renewed ability to initiate the previously authorized $100 million share repurchase program.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Precision Drilling GAAP EPS of $1.34 misses by $0.41, revenue of $526M beats by $154.45M
data.description:
• Precision Drilling press release (PDS): Q1 GAAP EPS of $1.34 misses by $0.41.
  • Revenue of $526M (+6.0% Y/Y) beats by $154.45M.
  • Cash provided by operations was $63 million and the Company repurchased 36,874 shares for $4 million and reduced long-term debt by $25 million. Precision ended the quarter with $41 million of cash and more than $430 million in available liquidity.
  • Adjusted EBITDA decreased 10% to $124 million from $137 million in the first quarter of 2025. The decrease was primarily due to higher share-based compensation expense of $19 million compared with $3 million in the same period last year, as well as increased rig reactivation costs. For additional information on share-based compensation, please refer to "Other Items" later in this news release.

data.title: NorthWestern Non-GAAP EPS of $1.31 beats by $0.02, revenue of $497.6M misses by $4.48M
data.description:
• NorthWestern press release (NWE): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $1.31 beats by $0.02.
  • Revenue of $497.6M (+6.6% Y/Y) misses by $4.48M.
  • Affirms 2026 earnings guidance range of $3.68 to $3.83 per diluted share.
  • Affirms record $683 million capital plan for 2026 and 4% to 6% long-term EPS and rate base growth rate.
  • Announces $0.67 per share quarterly dividend - payable June 30, 2026.
